pr.title: Decompile Big Blue stage functions (grbigblue, grbigblueroute)
pr.author: malvarezcastillo

summary: Large Big Blue stage decompilation PR covering grbigblue.c and grbigblueroute.c, with 10 files changed and nearly 3k added lines. It replaced many placeholder `/// #symbol` entries with C implementations, added Big Blue-specific static declarations/types, exposed needed prototypes in ground/mp/grfzerocar headers, and improved names for local variables and callbacks. The PR included several partial or near-matching functions, with explicit TODO comments documenting match percentages and remaining assembly issues such as register allocation, stack offsets, and addressing mode differences. Human discussion was minimal; the only excerpted comment noted that partial matches represented substantial progress even if the bot did not count them fully.
what_changed: Decompiled numerous Big Blue stage functions in `src/melee/gr/grbigblue.c`, including stage initialization, ground object setup, platform/item logic, callback registration, JObj transform setup, and cleanup paths.
Decompiled functions in `src/melee/gr/grbigblueroute.c`, with the PR context specifically calling out `grBigBlueRoute_8020C85C` as a 91.77% partial match.
Added `src/melee/gr/grbigblue.static.h` with Big Blue-specific static declarations/types, including symbols such as `grBb_LineIds`, `grBb_YakumonoParams`, and track-related entries.
Expanded shared ground/stage types in `src/melee/gr/types.h` to support Big Blue state fields used through `GET_GROUND(gobj)->gv.bigblue` and raw offset accesses where types were still incomplete.
Updated public headers for `grbigblue`, `grbigblueroute`, `grfzerocar`, `ground`, `mpcoll`, and `mplib` to expose functions and callback signatures needed by the new C implementations.
Added static Big Blue data in C such as `grBb_803B8108`, `grBb_803B8114`, `grBb_TrackEntries`, and external references including `grBb_803B8120`, `grBb_803B8134`, and `grBb_804D69C8`.
Replaced generic argument names in several existing functions with semantic names such as `gobj`, `gp`, `jobj`, `jobj1` through `jobj5`, and `gp2`.

Added includes for dependencies required by the newly decompiled logic, including `grfzerocar.h`, `ground.h`, `baselib/random.h`, `gm/gm_1A45.h`, item headers, `lbvector.h`, `mplib.h`, `math_ppc.h`, `math.h`, and `trigf.h`.
smart_moves: Kept near-matches in the tree with explicit `@TODO` comments and current match percentages, making future work searchable and avoiding loss of partially recovered logic.

Documented specific blockers in TODO comments, for example register allocation, stack offset differences, pointer advancement, loop/index variable shape, and PowerPC addressing mode differences such as `lwzx/stwx` versus `add+lwz/stw`.
Used `PAD_STACK` and `FORCE_PAD_STACK` around functions where stack layout affected matching, showing an intentional matching tactic rather than purely cosmetic C cleanup.
Introduced a Big Blue static header rather than overloading the public header with internal stage-only structures and statics.
Normalized pointer cleanup from integer zero to `NULL` after freeing Big Blue heap allocations, improving C clarity while preserving intended behavior.
Added assertions after heap allocation and before dereferencing important JObjs, mirroring expected runtime checks and helping match original assert sites.
Preserved raw offset accesses where the exact struct layout was not yet fully modeled, instead of inventing unsupported field names.
Centralized access to Big Blue runtime parameters through `grBb_804D69C8 = Ground_801C49F8()` and reused it for transforms, scaling, and randomized timing/range values.
decomp_lessons: For large stage decompilation PRs, landing high-quality partial matches with clear TODOs can be valuable progress even before every function reaches 100%.
When a stage has many map object variants, split public prototypes from static/internal stage structs so future work can refine types without polluting public headers.
Use semantic local names for common engine objects (`gobj`, `gp`, `jobj`, `coll`) once roles are known; this makes large stage functions more reviewable and easier to continue.
If struct layout is only partially understood, raw offset accesses can be acceptable as temporary evidence-preserving code, but should be paired with follow-up type refinement.
Stage matching often requires reproducing original heap allocation sizes, table indexing, JObj child/next traversal, and callback registration order exactly.
Explicitly recording match percentages and assembly mismatches in comments makes later decomp sessions more efficient because the next contributor can target the known compiler-shape issue.
Randomized behavior should preserve original range handling, including equal/min/max cases and `HSD_Randi` guards for zero ranges.
JObj transform setup order matters for matching and behavior: this PR consistently sets translate/scale/rotation through HSD setters rather than direct field writes where appropriate.
naming_conventions: Big Blue symbols use the project’s stage prefix style: public functions use `grBigBlue_801E....`, route functions use `grBigBlueRoute_8020....`, and static/data symbols use `grBb_....`.
Ground object parameters were renamed from generic `arg0`/`arg` to `gobj` when the value is a `Ground_GObj*`.
Ground user-data locals were named `gp`, matching common decomp shorthand for `Ground*`.
HSD joint object locals were named `jobj`, `child`, `next`, `cur`, or numbered `jobj1` through `jobj5` depending on role.
Big Blue-specific struct/type names in the context use `grBb_` prefixes, such as `grBb_YakumonoParams`, `grBb_LineIds`, and `grBb_TrackEntry`.
Internal helper callbacks retain generated names such as `fn_801E8560` and `fn_801E6124` when no stronger semantic name is evidenced.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: Used `PAD_STACK(...)` in functions such as `grBigBlue_801E57C0`, `grBigBlue_801E5B20`, `grBigBlue_801E613C`, `fn_801E8560`, and `grBigBlue_801E8794` to influence stack layout.
Used `FORCE_PAD_STACK(16)` in `grBigBlue_801E8D64`, indicating a stronger stack-shaping requirement for matching.

Left comments identifying non-final matches: `grBigBlue_801E6904` at 99.98%, `fn_801E8560` at 87.65%, `grBigBlue_801E8794` at 90.70%, `grBigBlue_801E8A1C` at 79.9%, and `grBigBlue_801E8B84` at 94.71% in the excerpt.

Used raw pointer arithmetic against `Ground*` and `tmpPadData` for unresolved layout regions, for example offsets around `0xC8`, `0xCC`, `0xD0`, `0xD4`, `0x134`, `0x188`, and `tmpPadData + 0x44C/0x488`.
Matched original child traversal/reparenting logic by walking `HSD_JObjGetChild` and `HSD_JObjGetNext`, then calling `HSD_JObjReparent` and HSD transform setters.
Replicated original random-call ordering in `grBigBlue_801E8D04` by storing four `HSD_Randf()` calls into temporaries before calling `Ground_801C38D0` with reversed argument order.
Used explicit casts for callback registration, such as `(mpLib_Callback) fn_801E8560`, to satisfy headers while matching expected engine callback wiring.

Included TODO note that `grBigBlue_801E8A1C` still differed because the generated platform/store code used `lwzx/stwx` instead of `add+lwz/stw`, and candidate pointer initialization missed a `slwi+add` shape.

review_feedback.feedback: A commenter noted that the bot did not count partial matches in the total, despite there being 'a ton of progress' in the PR.
review_feedback.actionable_lesson: Do not undervalue partial stage decompilation work when TODOs identify the remaining match blockers.

review_feedback.feedback: No review comments were present in the provided slice.
review_feedback.actionable_lesson: No specific reviewer-requested naming, structure, or code changes can be inferred from this PR context.

follow_up_queries: Find later PRs touching `src/melee/gr/grbigblue.c` TODO comments for `grBigBlue_801E6904`, `fn_801E8560`, `grBigBlue_801E8794`, `grBigBlue_801E8A1C`, or `grBigBlue_801E8B84`.
Search for later refinements to `grBb_YakumonoParams`, `grBb_LineIds`, `grBb_TrackEntry`, and Big Blue fields in `src/melee/gr/types.h`.
Check whether raw offsets in Big Blue ground state, especially `0xC8`, `0xCC`, `0xD0`, `0xD4`, `0x134`, `0x188`, and `0x1DC`, were replaced by named struct fields.

Look for future decompilation of the functions listed in the PR body as partial: `grBigBlue_801E6364`, `grBigBlue_801E8D64`, `grBigBlue_801EB4AC`, `grBigBlue_801ED694`, `grBigBlue_801EDF44`, and `grBigBlueRoute_8020C85C`.
Search for matching tactics involving `PAD_STACK`, `FORCE_PAD_STACK`, `lwzx/stwx`, and pointer advancement in other stage PRs.
